title: Cat Screaming When Picked Up Under Belly: Emergency Guide
meta_desc: If your cat screams when lifted by the abdomen, they are in acute pain. Learn about potential causes and why this is a high-priority emergency.
content_html:
Understanding Acute Abdominal Pain

When a cat vocalizes or screams specifically when touched or lifted under the belly, it is a significant clinical sign of acute pain. Because cats are masters at hiding discomfort, a scream indicates that the pain has become overwhelming. This reaction typically suggests an issue with the internal organs (such as the bladder, kidneys, or intestines) or a severe issue with the spine and surrounding musculature.

Urgency Level: High

This situation is a high-priority veterinary emergency. Vocalizing from abdominal pressure can be a sign of life-threatening conditions including a urinary blockage (especially common and dangerous in male cats), acute pancreatitis, peritonitis, or internal trauma. You should contact your nearest emergency veterinarian immediately, as many of these conditions can lead to rapid decline if not treated within hours.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

If it is safe to do so without causing more pain, a quick video of your cat's posture or their reaction can provide vital clues to the triage staff. Seeing whether your cat is 'guarding' their belly, walking with a hunched back (kyphosis), or straining to urinate helps the veterinary team determine the level of urgency and prepare the necessary diagnostic tools—such as ultrasound or X-rays—before you even arrive.

faqs.question: What are the most common causes of abdominal pain in cats?
faqs.answer: Common causes include urinary tract obstructions, severe constipation or megacolon, pancreatitis, ingestion of a foreign object, or internal infections like pyometra in unspayed females.

faqs.question: Should I try to palpate the belly to find the source of pain?
faqs.answer: No. Avoid pressing on the abdomen as this can cause further injury, potentially rupture a delicate organ like the bladder, or cause the cat to bite out of fear and pain.

faqs.question: How should I move my cat to the vet?
faqs.answer: Try to minimize contact with the belly. Use a 'towel wrap' or gently slide the cat into a carrier by tilting the carrier or using a flat board to avoid putting pressure on the sensitive abdominal area.
